title: Listening - Part 3: Rewriting the Code of Life
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a geneticist, Dr. Abbi, and a philosopher, Noah, about the implications of CRISPR technology. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding 'germline editing,' the eradication of hereditary diseases, and the divide between 'therapy' and 'enhancement.'
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We now have the molecular scissors to edit our own DNA. But just because we can, does it mean we should? Joining me are Dr. Abbi, a leader in genome editing, and Noah, an ethics professor. Abbi, how close are we to 'curing' genetic diseases?

Abbi: [15] We are already seeing incredible results with 'somatic' editing—treating cells in a living patient to fix things like sickle cell anemia. [/15] The real controversy is 'germline' editing, which changes the DNA of embryos. Those changes are permanent and are passed down to every future generation. It’s a profound responsibility because we are essentially taking control of human evolution away from nature.

Interviewer: Noah, where do you draw the line between fixing a problem and 'improving' a human being?

Noah: [16] That is the billion-dollar question. Most people agree on 'therapy'—eliminating a fatal heart defect, for instance. But 'enhancement'—increasing IQ, height, or athletic ability—is a moral minefield. [/16] If we allow the wealthy to purchase 'superior' genes for their children, we risk creating a biological caste system that could never be undone. Inequality would no longer be just economic; it would be encoded in our cells.

Interviewer: Abbi, is there a risk of 'unintended consequences' with CRISPR?

Abbi: [17] Biology is rarely a simple 'cut and paste.' A gene that causes a disease might also provide protection against something else. [/17] If we eliminate a specific trait, we might accidentally make the human race more vulnerable to a future virus. We don't yet have a full map of the 'genetic ecosystem' within our own bodies.

Interviewer: Noah, some argue that parents have a 'moral obligation' to give their children the best possible start. What do you think?

Noah: [18] That’s a dangerous path. If we treat children like 'products' that can be customized, we lose the idea of 'unconditional love.' [/18] A child becomes a project to be optimized rather than a person to be discovered. We should be changing society to accommodate different types of people, not changing people to fit a narrow definition of 'success.'

Interviewer: Abbi, is a global ban on certain types of editing even possible?

Abbi: [19] It’s very difficult. Science doesn't respect borders. If one country bans 'designer babies' but another allows them, we’ll see 'genetic tourism.' [/19] We need an international consensus, but currently, different cultures have very different views on the sanctity of the genome.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Noah? Are we ready for this power?

Noah: [20] Our technological power is growing exponentially, but our wisdom is not. We are like toddlers playing with a hand grenade. We need to slow down and ask what kind of humanity we actually want to preserve. [/20]

Interviewer: Abbi, Noah, thank you.

questions.question: Why does Abbi find 'germline' editing more concerning than 'somatic' editing?
options: A) It is much more expensive to perform on patients.
B) The results of the procedure are only temporary.
C) The changes made are inherited by all future descendants.
D) It has not yet been tested on any living organisms.
questions.explanation: She states that changes to embryos are permanent and passed down to every future generation.

questions.question: Noah warns that 'genetic enhancement' could lead to
options: A) a world where everyone is equally talented and intelligent.
B) a new type of permanent social inequality based on biology.
C) the complete disappearance of the middle class.
D) a faster way for humans to migrate to other planets.
questions.explanation: He mentions the risk of a 'biological caste system' if the wealthy can buy 'superior' genes.

questions.question: Abbi suggests that removing certain genes might be dangerous because
options: A) those genes might also serve a hidden, protective function.
B) the process of editing always causes physical pain to the patient.
C) viruses will eventually learn how to edit their own DNA.
D) it will make humans too heavy to walk properly.
questions.explanation: She notes that a gene causing a disease might also protect against something else.

questions.question: In Noah's opinion, customizing children's traits might
options: A) help parents understand their children much better.
B) force schools to change their teaching methods.
C) change the fundamental nature of the parent-child relationship.
D) result in children who are much happier than their parents.
questions.explanation: He argues that treating children as 'customizable products' means losing the idea of 'unconditional love'.

questions.question: Abbi believes that a global ban on gene editing would be difficult because
options: A) the technology is too cheap for anyone to control.
B) people would simply travel to countries with fewer regulations.
C) all world leaders agree that it is a good idea.
D) scientists have already decided to ignore all laws.
questions.explanation: She mentions 'genetic tourism' as a consequence of inconsistent international laws.

questions.question: Noah's final metaphor about the 'hand grenade' suggests that
options: A) technology is a weapon that should only be used in war.
B) humans are currently too immature to handle such powerful tools.
C) the technology will eventually explode and destroy the planet.
D) we should give this technology to everyone immediately.
questions.explanation: He compares us to 'toddlers' with a grenade, implying a lack of wisdom to manage the power.
